Dear JMG,

Thanks as always for hosting this forum. I know you often share occult tips on the art of memory... any specific advice from that perspective on learning a foreign language?

Best,
Ryan M.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The art of memory isn't really well suited for that. The best method I know of is one that the Sâr Peladan talks about in one of his novels. Get the same text in a language you know and the language you're trying to learn, and memorize it sentence by sentence, by rote, in both languages. That will speed up the process of absorbing the grammar and structure of the new language, as well as the vocabulary, to an astonishing degree.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks very much, that’s a great suggestion!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you can find a station that broadcasts in the language you are learning,listen to the radio whenever you can.  Commercials are your friends.  The message is simple and there is much repetition.  Also listen to news (and there still is plenty of news in, for example, Spanish, unlike in English where it’s all propaganda).  Listen for the words that turn up in connection with public figures.  Before you know it, you’ll understand much of the news!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Another tip here along the same lines: learn to pronounce the new language as perfectly as possible first, before learning any vocabulary or grammar.

The day I saw the smurf-torture, later in the day, I was standing in line at the store, 6 feet from everyone, staring vacantly into space as I waited. Suddenly, my mental screen came on with a vision of General Nuisance sitting at home, minding his own business. Out of the corner of his eye he sees something blue-and-white scurry across the floor. He pegs a heavy candlestick at it but misses. He calls out into the next room. “Upala! We’ve got smurfs again!” (When they were young and poor, Mrs. Nuisance was an exterminator.). End of scene.

The line moved, we all shuffled forward 6 feet, and as I shuffled I wondered, “Man, did the Marquis de Sade get his ideas this way?” 😳

replies.title: Re: The Dolmen Arch questions
replies.content:
1) For the Dolmen Arch work, I recommend choosing a set of powers to invoke from a single cultural and religious background. That doesn't stop you from invoking anyone you want in your other spiritual practices -- quite the contrary! But the rituals work best if the deities you invoke are from a single coherent tradition.

2) The great thing about polytheism is that you can do that sort of thing without the least difficulty. Your ancient Greek polytheist, for example, could make offerings to Belenos and Epona while traveling in Gaul and seek initiation into the mysteries of the Cabeiri while visiting Samothrace, without that interfering in the least with his worship of Zeus, Demeter, Athena et al. at home in Athens. You're in exactly the same situation -- you can practice the rites of this set of Druid mysteries, and invoke such gods and goddesses as you see fit, while comtinuing your relationships with your deities.

comments.title: Electional astrology
comments.content:
It sounds like there are some baleful charts coming up in the next couple of months. If there are some useful aspects to a natal chart, but the general chart for the time is bad, which takes precedence, assuming the event can't wait a long time?

To what extent can a bad aspect in a natal chart be cancelled out by a good transit?

replies.title: Re: Electional astrology
replies.content: Your natal chart always takes precedence over everything else. Transits don't add anything new to the chart -- they simply tell you when the influences set out in your natal chart are going to happen. (The same is true of progressed charts.) Say Mars in your birth chart is well dignified and in good aspect to other planets. If Saturn squares it by transit, that doesn't mean something dreadful will happen, it just means that the positive indications expressed by your natal Mars will have to contend with obstacles.  Equally, if (let's say) your natal Venus is afflicted by position and aspect, and the Moon trines her, that doesn't erase the problems with your emotional life predicted by the Venus placement, it just makes them easier to deal with for a while.

comments.title: A small geomancy-ritual

Just a data point this week: Months ago I posted about performing a geomantic reading in a ritualistic way: Draw the chart clockwise and outward to inward and invoke whatever guidance and assistance seems appropriate. Then cast and interpret the chart and afterwards re-draw the lines of the chart, inward to outward and counterclockwise. While doing so, thank and release the energies you have invoked.

I'm doing this for a while now, mixed with "normal" readings without the ritualistic component. It does seem to work and to improve the "signal to noise ratio" of the reading quite a bit. Additionally, it helps to focus on the casting and reading more tightly. Can't compare it to what it feels like if you do a full banishing ritual before divination, though. So, if you can't do or don't want to do the full "occult trinity" for whatever reason, this at least seems to be worth a try and it also combines nicely with burning incense and prayer before performing this small ritual.

Nachtgurke

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

This is more of a question for the other blog, but it has an occult dimension so I thought I would post it here. One of the major themes of your work is that “progress isn’t real,” and you’ve criticized the Western world for living under a “myth of progress” that functions like a secular religion. However, this assertion clashes with the observation that progress is clearly real in some areas, some of the time. This is easily proved by examples. For better or worse, computing power has increased exponentially since the 1960’s (I’m not saying that this can last indefinitely, or that it's necessarily a good thing). Dentistry is better today than it was in the time of the barber surgeons. Infant mortality is significantly lower on average than it was throughout most of history. Do you believe that these gains are illusory in some way?

My understanding is that the “myth of progress” requires some infinite extrapolation into the future of current trends like a law of nature, which is disproved by the historical record – civilizations collapse, gains are rolled back, and so on. But to me, that’s not enough to dismiss progress in the short term, in specific areas that can improve human life. Human societies are dynamic, not static. We do learn our lessons, and we can move from a suboptimal state to a more optimal state, even if perfection is never achieved. This constant adjustment to circumstances and striving towards the manifestation of values, sometimes achieving spectacular success and other times not, constitutes “progress.”

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a source of wry amusement to me that I get asked this identical question over and over again, and no matter how loudly I splash the answer across the internet, somehow it never sinks in. Let's try it again.

First, you're using the modern degraded notion of what the word "myth" means, and I'm not. You think that a myth is a story that's not true. The older, richer meaning is of course that a myth is a story that people use to project ideas of meaning, purpose, and value onto the universe. Progress is a myth in the latter sense because people use it as a basis for their ideas of meaning, purpose, and value; they place their own lives in the context of the supposed onward and upward march of progress.

Second, you're cherrypicking examples of situations where progress has arguably happened, and using those to bolster the claim that "we do learn our lessons" -- that is to say, that history really does move from worse to better over time. (That's the central claim of the myth of progress.) The cherrypicking is essential, because regress also happens -- for example, infant mortality in the United States has been increasing for decades, and current rates here are roughly equivalent to those in Indonesia -- and for every example of progress you can name, it would be quite easy to give an example of regress.

Now the usual gambit of believers in progress, faced by this, is to say that this may be true but we're not as bad off as people in the Dark Ages. That's true, and it points out the final dimension of what I'm trying to say. Civilizations rise, and then they fall. Ours has traced out just over half that arc, and so yes, at the moment, we're well above dark age conditions. What this ignores, and what the entire myth of progress is set up to ignore, is that western industrial civilization peaked most of a century ago and is well into the early stages of decline.

Has technology advanced since the mid-20th century? Sure, but technology is a lagging indicator; Roman civilization was still progressing technologically when the barbarians swept over it. (One of the features of barbarians that doesn't get much media time is that they're very good at figuring out how to use or monkeywrench new technologies.) Your ideology fails to realize that not only can we move from a suboptimal state to an even less optimal state, and keep on going, but we are in fact doing that right now. That unraveling of attempts to adjust to circumstances and failing to manifest values, sometimes suffering spectacular disasters and sometimes not, constitutes the future ahead of us -- and the myth of progress offers no help at all in dealing with that harsh but inescapable reality.

replies.content: Exactly. Individual souls evolve and develop, and as they do so, they move from one state of being to another. comments.title: Ritual magic in dream spaces
comments.content:
So, last week I asked your thoughts on the safety of attempting ritual magic in a lucid dream space, starting with the LIRP (since it seems like it would stand to reason that the LBRP would just banish the dream and wake me up like it usually does when my passive dreaming self does it spontaneously as a defensive reaction). I had not expected the opportunity to present itself this week, since I can neither control or predict when a lucid dream is going to happen. I can work on training my waking mind to be mindful of patterns and textures, object permanence, and the behavior of physical objects but it’s up to my passive dreaming mind to take advantage of that, notice its dreaming, and become active without waking up my body. Well… it happened, and left me with some questions (note, I’m not asking about dream interpretation or symbolism, I tried to weed out the passive ordinary dreams as much as possible, but the basics are still there for context).

Wednesday night, I was in the midst of a rather unpleasant dream involving killer alligators, violent police and attack dogs, and ravenous walking corpses... at one point in the dream I flew into the air to escape a shambling corpse and my mind caught on to the physical impossibility of flying, and I suddenly found myself conscious within the dream space. One thing that always happens when my conscious mind awakens inside of a dream is that most of the environments and ambient patterns and all of the “dream NPCs” vanish leaving me alone in a completely barren environment. My guess is that this has something to do with the processing power it takes the subconscious to maintain a dream environment for a passive versus an active awareness. I therefore found myself alone on an empty street.

Since the dream itself had been fairly violent and stressful, I decided to do the LBRP first, not knowing whether that would wake me up or not, but not wanting to invoke in a place that had moments ago been rife with shambling corpses and attack dogs.
As I performed the ritual, the dream space re-arranged, melted, and shifted around me, and once I’d finished I was inside of a rectangular room with a deep azure colored floor, a golden ceiling, one wall painted sky blue with a childlike finger painting of a sun and moon, one black wall, one orange wall, and a window that revealed a twilit garden on the other side. From here, I went ahead and started performing the LIRP. One interesting effect of the ritual was that every word I said was echoed by a chanting female voice trickling in through what sounded like a PA system. Over the course of the ritual, I began to feel like I was pushing against enormous resistance with every movement I took. As the ritual went on the resistance got more intense and the colors and images began to fade to black until during the final cabalistic cross, I became fully aware of my body in bed, and sat upright. I did not awaken into the physical world, but into another dream environment (once more my mind was passive). I was in a bed in an old trailer home that looked like something from the ‘70s with walls and carpets of a dingy brownish yellow color. Sitting by my bedside were a teenage boy with clean cut black hair in a blue flannel shirt and a tall middle aged woman with long black hair and a yellow dress with a white apron. They interviewed me about everything I had experienced. Then, after I had finished talking the woman went to the kitchen to prepare breakfast. I then woke up.

I woke up with my heart pounding, breathing heavily with exertion. I felt like I’d just run a marathon. When I tried to get out of bed I found my whole body felt like Jelly, and as I walked my legs were shaking. The shaking and the feelings of weakness lasted for about an hour and a half, until I’d completed my morning rituals and eaten breakfast. Once I ate they subsided pretty readily and I felt fine through the rest of the day. My next night’s sleep was completely dreamless and the next two nights after that I had normal, mundane dreams.

With this, I feel kind of like I’m setting out into somewhat unmapped territory in terms of noticing what to watch out for and what to expect moving forward. The physical aftermath seemed sufficiently like the effects you’d said to watch out for with inner plane workings near the end of LRM, so I imagine I’d need to follow the same precautions you’d recommend for someone who over-exerted themselves doing one of those (lots of food and water, avoiding alcohol for the rest of the day, and holding off on intensive visualization work for 3 days). However, the intensity of the exhaustion was unlike anything I’ve ever experienced doing magic, and there are some patent differences between doing work in a dream space and doing work in a visualized environment. For one, of course, it takes tremendous effort just to be there, much less perform active ritual magic there since it’s an environment that’s used to housing a passive dreaming mind rather than a conscious one. However, the dream space is more tangible, more solid, denser, and I have no awareness of my physical body at all. I have no control over the environment, it clearly does respond to ritual, but not in ways I can predict or control. One thing I’d kind of expected is that visualization would be easier in the dream space, since both visualizations and dreams are astral, however when doing the ritual the visualizations were just as difficult to do and just as ephemeral as they are when I’m doing physical ritual, appearing faintly in my mind’s eye rather than manifesting in the dream environment.

Overall, it seems like I’m working with a different level of being from the astral environment of an imagined space… something considerably denser (since Yesod is tied to both the etheric plane and to dreams, would it be reasonable to suggest that there’s a component of the dream space that is etheric in substance and not just astral, which might explain some of those differences?). So, I suppose in short the question is, just how novel is this territory? It seems like such an obvious thing to try, that I’d expect it to be widely practiced and written about, but I’ve never seen dreams talked about this way (as a space for active work, rather than as a passive state of consciousness), and most books on Lucid Dreaming tend to look at it from either a new age, or pop psychology perspective, so aren't going to be talking about using them for ritual magic. Is there any map to this territory, any known risks? Or is this something I’d need to explore through trial and experiment, treading very carefully?

comments.title: Questions on Alchemy
comments.content:
JMG,

I've been thinking some more about alchemy, and I have a few questions.

1) Re: 'lead into gold' A lot of the popular pablum of alchemy today is centered around the concept that the Work was the well-intentioned misguided attempts of naive experimenters at proto-chemistry and charlatans (of course, this is partly true). But as far as I understand, transmuting these two particular metals wasn't always an overriding theme through history or other cultures. Is this correct, and if so, when did 'lead into gold' first start appearing and/or dominating the literature? Or was this more an outside perception of the Art?

1a) Did it happen to be around the same time that lead bullets helped to transport massive new quantities of gold from the New World to the Old?

2) The Philosopher's Stone is uniquely connected to the philosopher, and his or her mental, emotional, and physical state. It can't simply be handed over to someone who didn't do the work themselves, one must walk the Royal Path alone. The First Material is related to the personal Imagination of the alchemist. Is this line of thinking congruent with what you know?

3) Coincidentia oppositorum seems to be the core method of the process in much of the literature. It also seems that Taoism could almost be thought of as a sort of alchemical religion. Thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Questions on Alchemy
replies.content:
1) Lead into gold is a popular misapprehension. The historically attested examples of transmutation all involved turning mercury (element 80) into gold (element 79). The metaphoric "transmutation" of lead bullets into gold through the Red Work of massive bloodshed might have helped feed that misapprehension, yes.

2) It depends very much on which kind of alchemy you're talking about. The powder of metallic transmutation could in fact be given to another person -- again, the historically attested examples include cases where this was done. As for the First Matter, the texts are unanimous in saying that it's everywhere, and treated with scorn by those who have no idea what it's value is.

3) Er, were you not aware that Taoism has its own massive and richly developed alchemical traditions?

replies.title: Re: Questions on Alchemy
replies.content:

comments.title: magic and nonviolence
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer! I have 2 of your books (currently reading Monsters) and very much appreciate the quality of your writing.

I've become very interested in magic, both natural and ritual, but I find myself troubled by a question about it. In ritual magic it appears that the magician sometimes invokes and commands spiritual beings of different kinds, including angels and archangels. Are these beings not much higher and more powerful than we are? How is it possible to give them orders, and is it ethical?

I am a Buddhist, and have taken the Bodhisattva vow to work for the enlightenment of all beings. I'm concerned that Western ritual magic may be based on a culture of compulsion, or anyway disrespect, toward the nonhuman entities with which it deals.

Can Western ritual magic be practiced by a Buddhist who is nonviolent and committed only to the ultimate welfare of all beings? What's the right approach for such a person?

replies.title: Re: magic and nonviolence
replies.content:
This is a very common question, but it's based on a misunderstanding of classic Western occultism that's been fostered by some recent fringe traditions. The current fad for grimoire magic tends to foster this, since the grimoires of the late Middle Ages are very much into the culture of compulsion you rightly mistrust. The thing to keep in mind is that the grimoires were pop-culture books, right up there alongside Rhonda Byrne's The Secret. (Many of them make the same claims about getting lots of money without working for it!)

In classic wWstern occultism, by contrast, you don't give orders to angels and archangels, and you command demons only when it's necessary to make them stop harming someone. (As I recall, many Buddhist masters of the past were tolerably good at this, too!) In the Golden Dawn tradition, in which I had my original training, and in the Druid teachings where I trained more recently, work with spirits is always carried out on the basis of mutual respect and a clear understanding of humanity's not very exalted position in the scheme of things.

The Golden Dawn papers literally stress that you have no right to harm a demon -- just to send it on its way when it's messing with you or others. So you may be favorably surprised if you look into the traditional lore.

I just finished reading your book on polytheism and properly enjoyed it. replies.title: Re: Polytheism
replies.content: We don't know. Repeat that until it sinks in: We don't know. The chaos magicians are whistling in the dark with the rest of us. Theology is a collection of speculations about color by people who were born blind and have never experienced light. Do deities come into being because people invoke them? Do people suddenly decide to invoke a deity because a new deity has arrived on the scene and calls them? We don't know. All we know is that something happens in response to some religious rituals, and what happens varies with the ritual, the deity, the state of the worshipers, and a galaxy of other variables.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG. Cradle catholic here. I just wanted to draw your attention to how you just now started "believing in a world of spirits" but you "usually pray the rosary or to your guardian angel" and accept that those work. Good topic for meditation, that ;)

As someone who struggled with similar anxiety for a year or two, I conclude most of it comes from being raised in an environment where all the invisible spiritual things have an answer you can more or less look up in a God-approved book, and the answer is typically "thing is of God so it's cool" or "thing is not of God, get it behind thee." All the ambiguity is wrapped up in humans who have the advantage of being tangible so while you may not know their true intentions, you at least know for sure they exist and what their actions are.

But now that there are these "spirits" that are invisible, ambiguous, and not really covered in the books with the God-approved answers, it puts you in a situation of having to figure things out about invisible, ambiguous, spirits on your own, and the answer isn't "its good" or "its bad" but more "it depends" or "kind of yellowish-purple", which is not a situation you're used to being in as far as religion is concerned.

Plus there's that part of Christianity we're typically raised with where getting the wrong answer meant you would go someplace very warm for an indefinite period of time. The sudden ambiguity plus the lack of tools to deal with it plus the perceived high risk of being wrong can combine for a good deal of anxiety.

Best way to deal with it is to reaffirm your trust that God has your back and ask him to help guide you to more joyfully interact with whatever parts of the spiritual side of His creation that you need to interact with. It should get easier once you remember you're not in this alone, the Good Shepherd doesn't just let his sheep wander into Gehenna by accident, and even if you do somehow manage it, there's sacraments and holy water for that.

I know occult (mis)attribution was sometimes to give the books a greater sense of gravitas, and seeing the man behind the curtain may disenchant readers of that. It also may be a matter of Reality Tunnels (in Robert Anton Wilson's terms) where mental paradigms can be important.
It seems like it would be a good experiment, but it is impossible to read the same book the first time twice.

replies.title: Re: Reading as from (mis)attributed authors vs. actual
replies.content: Good question. I don't tend to worry about the authors; my interest is in the material they pass on -- but of course your mileage may vary.

comments.title: CGD Questions, Coch and Gwyn

Hello JMG!

I hope you are well, and thank you for the clarification of the Elemental Associations with Direction, Complexity, Flow, and Particularity. That helped a lot.

I'm continuing my way through the Ovate grade of the CGD. Opening the Temple, Praying, Meditating, Closing, and moving on. I've also noticed that whenever I miss a day, I lose the "Momentum" and he feeling of "Sweetness" I feel building up from previous practice. Oof. I'm thus rearranging various life things to better enable my magical practice on the days where it's been tough.

Generally as I've practiced, I've been doing the set meditation till I run out of new material, then starting to continue whatever the astral exercises from that phase are at the beginning of my meditation, then meditating on various other things, like the mythology, figures, etc. As I review and go back, I often notice things I was previously doing wrong, and try to adjust my practices. (This is really teaching me how often I miss components of directions) One thing I've not been doing consistently since the beginning, is adequately working in the Natural Breath to my meditation, and the journaling of Ideas and images afterwards. With all the ritual and prayer and the opening of the temple, my daily practice runs often as long as an hour and 15 minutes. By the end I'm usually rushing to get on with whatever I was next going to do, and that makes journaling difficult, and I think unfortunately I also have some kind of emotional blockage around post-practice journaling I need to look into.

And a somewhat separate question

2. Some of the elemental associations for the geomantic figures don't make sense to me. Most Specifically Coch and Gwyn. Gwyn is stated to be related to mind, clarity, is good for gain, etc, and Coch is said to be related to the body, passion, intoxication, and confusion. Associated with mind, Communication, complexity, and this is not mentioned in the CGD (And perhaps is part of my confusion), but my previous training involving elements and Tarot makes me equate air with swords, and clarity, and distinctions. Something I associate much more easily with Air, rather than water.

I want to assign Water to Coch (Though that's not the formulation listed in the book) because I associate water with being less distinct, more murky, with passion, and emotion, and being closer to the body/physical (earth) than the mind.
